definitions: Literal: literally, 'prince of eggs'; a figurative image without a real physical referent.
Literal translation of the proverb 'Necessity is the mother of invention.'
Figurative: to be able to take action or operate effectively; to have the capacity or mobility to manage tasks or affairs.
To make a fool of someone; to deceive, trick, or treat someone as foolish.
Distant drums sound pleasant; a literal observation that sounds from afar may seem attractive.
Literal: if the head is intact, a thousand turbans are possible.
To become suddenly alert or startled awake, often as a result of a noise, thought, or strong emotion.

Figurative: one's safety or survival is more important than external honors or possessions; as long as the essential thing (life, reputation, or core interest) is preserved, material or decorative losses can be recovered.
Literal: a snake concealed within a sleeve or something that hides danger close to the body.
Literal: to be the eggplant on the plate; to be something merely present on a plate with no further implication.

Figurative: someone with limited knowledge, skill, or resources makes more noise, shows off, or causes more trouble than one who is full or competent; implies that superficial competence often leads to greater visible faults or boastfulness.
To see stars during the daytime; a literal, physically unlikely observation.
Figurative: Used to warn that a brief or ill‑found advantage or happiness will not last for someone who is soon likely to face consequences; implying that current rejoicing is premature.
idiom: बकरे की माँ कब तक ख़ैर मनायेगी

definitions: Figurative: The collection of hesitations, objections, or qualifications (the 'ifs and buts'); reservations or caveats presented in an argument or discussion.
Literal: To sing together with someone; to accompany another person or group in singing.
An observation that the workings of fate or divine illusion are unpredictable, bringing joy at some times and sorrow at others.
idiom: ईश्वर की माया कहीं धूप कहीं छाया

definitions: Figurative: completely or utterly; used for emphatic totality or thoroughness.
To become aware, enlightened, or consciously attentive to an issue, often used for moral, social, political, or spiritual awakening.
To do good deeds without being asked and without seeking recognition or reward; proactive, unprompted benevolence.
To warm one's hands in a house that is on fire.
Literal: an unpleasant sensation on the skin that causes a desire to scratch; itching.
To have the chest become physically broader.
Figurative: A proverb describing a person who is idle and unproductive but consumes resources; a good-for-nothing or freeloader who is a burden on provisions.
idiom: काम का न काज का दुश्मन अनाज का

definitions: When the source, means, or essential person is removed, the associated effects, functions, or benefits will also cease.
idiom: न रहेगा बाँस न बजेगी बाँसुरी

definitions: To charge, mount an attack, or advance to the front in battle or confrontation.
A figurative proverb meaning that rewards, bliss, or success require sacrifice, suffering, or great effort; nothing valuable is gained without giving something up.
A literal calm and stillness in the atmosphere immediately before a storm.
Figurative: One will reap the consequences of one’s deeds; people receive rewards or punishments proportionate to their actions (equivalent to 'you reap what you sow').
A rhetorical question asking, if a horse befriended grass, what would it eat, taken at face value.
idiom: घोड़ा घास से यारी करे तो खाए क्या?

definitions: Literal: Light or illumination; visible radiant energy that makes objects visible.
Figuratively, to deceive or trick someone; to make someone a fool for the speaker's advantage.
Literal: One dresses or equips oneself according to the customs or conditions of a country or place.
Literal: 'Ram's arrow' — a weapon associated with the god Ram in Hindu tradition.
Tears shed by a crocodile; the literal phenomenon of crocodile tears.
Literally, to have one's nose cut.
Figurative: to feel extreme irritation, exasperation, suffocation, or to be driven to the limits of patience or endurance.
The offspring or young of an owl; literally, a child of an owl.
The literal numeral thirty-six.
Literal: to stop breathing; to die.
Figurative: idle minds are prone to mischief or wrongdoing; when people are unoccupied, they are more likely to engage in bad or foolish behavior.
Literal: what is the use of regretting now that the bird has already eaten the field.
idiom: अब पछताए क्या होत जब चिड़िया चुग गई खेत

definitions: Literally, if there is life, there is the world; life makes everything else possible.
Figuratively, to be deceived, cheated, or made to look foolish; to be taken advantage of.
To feel depressed, disheartened, or dispirited; to be low in spirits.
Figurative: to be struck by a sudden calamity or catastrophe; to experience an abrupt disaster (as in the idiom 'आसमान फट जाना').
To state plainly the good and bad aspects of a person or situation, speaking frankly without softening the truth.
A rhetorical question literally meaning 'what would one not do when dying?', used to emphasize extreme necessity or compulsion.
Literal: if one plants a babul (acacia) tree, how will mangoes grow from it?
idiom: बोया पेड़ बबूल का तो आम कहाँ से होए

definitions: A term of endearment for a person regarded as very dear or beloved, often a child, close relative, or beloved companion.
To find a place for goods, funds, or resources; to allocate or settle assets or stock.
Figurative: to be extremely miserly or stingy; to avoid spending even a small amount of money at all costs.
idiom: चमड़ी जाए पर दमड़ी न जाए

definitions: A one-time lucky success or fluke that occurs without repeatable skill or reasoning.
To be overcome or deeply affected by an emotion or condition; to become immersed in a state (figurative use).
To take up, accept, or embrace an idea, practice, policy, role, or custom.
As a discourse marker: 'again' used to introduce a contrasting point or to return to a previous topic (equivalent to 'on the other hand' or 'moreover' in some contexts).
To narrowly escape a dangerous, difficult, or disastrous situation.
To confine one's zeal, support, or activity to one's own institution, group, or vested interest; to show partisan or self-interested behavior limited to one's own circle.
The tears that a crocodile sheds; literal tears produced by a crocodile.
To harm, betray, or take advantage of someone who has helped or supported you; equivalent to 'to bite the hand that feeds you.'
idiom: जिस थाली में खाना उसी में छेद करना

definitions: Literal: How long will the goat's mother celebrate?
idiom: बकरे की माँ कब तक ख़ैर मनायेगी

definitions: Figurative: To be in harmony or accord with someone or something; to act or feel in unison with another person or circumstance.
A statement describing a situation in which there are no grains at home while the mother goes out to roast them, taken at face value.
idiom: घर में नहीं दाने अम्मा चली भुनाने

definitions: Grapes that taste sour; fruit that is tart rather than sweet.
The principle or demand for equal retribution; seeking exact revenge or retributive justice ('an eye for an eye').
A conversational discourse marker used to resume, redirect, or re-emphasize a point in ongoing speech.
idiom: जैसा कि मैं कह रहा था

definitions: To become wet or soaked; to be drenched (literal use, typically of clothing or objects).
Figurative: to undertake great effort or make a big fuss and achieve only a trivial, disappointing, or negligible result; a disproportion between work and outcome.
Literally, to make someone chew chickpeas (chane) through their nose.
Literal (transitive verb): to give food to someone; to feed.
Literally, to not allow a fly to sit on one's nose.
idiom: नाक पर मक्खी न बैठने देना

definitions: Literal: A proverbial image stating that 'ghosts produced by kicks do not heed words,' i.e., a figurative formulation presented as a concrete image.
idiom: लातों के भूत बातों से नहीं मानते

definitions: To sprinkle salt on a burned area of the skin, a literal action that causes additional pain.
To entertain unrealistic hopes or fantasies; to be deluded or out of touch with reality.
Figurative: to fall quickly into someone's possession, control, or advantage; to be obtained or taken by someone almost instantly.
Literal: to have one's blood dry; to lose or have blood drained until it becomes dry or coagulated.
Literally, to have the term 'चूतिया' (a vulgar word meaning 'fool' or 'sucker') imposed or 'cut' on someone; not commonly used in a literal sense.
Figurative: to cease functioning or fail completely (applied to machines, plans, hopes, etc.).
Literal: a rhetorical question asking whether intelligence (mind) or a buffalo is larger.
To feel proud or elated; to experience swelling pride or self-satisfaction.
Figurative: To exhaust or overwork someone (or oneself) to the point of severe fatigue or breakage; to make someone work extremely hard.
To become aware or conscious of a fact, situation, or truth; to attain understanding or realization.
Used to assert that preserving life or health is the highest priority and that material possessions or other concerns are secondary.
Figurative (violent): to cause death by suffocation or to finish someone off (used colloquially).
Figurative: used to remark that people who are themselves flawed or unqualified still voice opinions or criticize others, implying hypocrisy or lack of credibility among those who speak.
idiom: सूप बोले तो बोले छलनी भी बोले जिसमें बहत्तर छेद

definitions: Literally, 'nine gems' or a collection of nine valuable jewels.
An emotional state of resentment, envy, or jealousy felt toward another person because of their advantages, successes, attention, or possessions.
To remain silent and endure an injustice, insult, or disadvantage without protest; to suffer passively because one cannot or does not speak up.
Literal retaliatory violence in which blood is avenged by blood; physical vengeance resulting in bloodshed.
Figurative: to make no difference; to have no effect or impact; to be of no avail.
Literal: to have the chest become physically broader.
To put someone in one's arms; to hug or embrace physically as an expression of affection or greeting.
Literal: The flour becomes wet when one is impoverished; a literal description of a poor household's misfortune.
Literal: 'Ram knows' — an assertion that the Hindu deity Ram is the one who knows or is aware of a matter.
Figuratively, to cheat, swindle, or defraud someone; to cause financial or reputational loss through deception or trickery.
Literal: in one's own lane or neighborhood even a dog appears like a lion.
idiom: अपनी गली में कुत्ता भी शेर होता है

definitions: Figurative: Contented with modest means; satisfied by basic necessities and not given to seeking luxury.
Figurative: to be petrified or horrified; to feel an intense, numbing fear or shock as if one’s blood has run cold.
Figurative: To cripple, devastate, or decisively defeat an opponent, organization, or capability; to destroy the core strength or backbone of something.
Literal: having dug a mountain, a mouse emerged.
Figurative: to pester, nag, or trouble someone persistently; to subject someone to repeated demands, indignity, or exploitation.
A proverbial statement that life under divine will alternates between sunshine and shade, indicating that fortune and adversity are fickle and transient.
idiom: ईश्वर की माया कहीं धूप कहीं छाया

definitions: To gain reputation, fame, recognition, or honor through achievement or notable actions.
Literal: To wash one's hands in the flowing Ganga (the Ganges River).
What is happening right now; an inquiry about current events or actions.
Literal: the image of a blind person unexpectedly catching or being handed a quail.
Literal: to remain silent; to not speak.
Figurative: to cooperate with, support, or join someone in an activity, opinion, or public stance.
Literal: If husband and wife are in agreement, what can the judge intervene to do?
idiom: मियाँ बीवी राज़ी तो क्या करेगा काज़ी

definitions: To receive pearls without asking, but not even alms when one asks (expressing the contrast between unexpected gifts and denied requests).
idiom: बिन माँगे मोती मिले माँगे मिले न भीख

definitions: Again; once more; indicating repetition of an action or event.
Good fortune or valuable favors sometimes arrive unasked, whereas actively asking for help may fail; blessings may come unexpectedly while requests go unanswered.
idiom: बिन माँगे मोती मिले माँगे मिले न भीख

definitions: To curdle or separate (as in milk turning sour and separating into curds and whey).
Figurative: people or things are not the same; differences in ability, quality, treatment, or worth should be expected.
Literal: Daily food or meals that sustain life; the ordinary food consumed day to day.
Literal: to nod repeatedly in agreement with someone.
To make a fist or strike with the fist.
An expression meaning 'only God knows,' used to indicate uncertainty or lack of knowledge about a matter.
Literal: what would a monkey know of the taste of ginger?
idiom: बंदर क्या जाने अदरक का स्वाद

definitions: Figurative: Livelihood or means of earning a living; the resources or work by which one secures basic subsistence.
Literal: To place something in its proper or designated location; to arrange or put away.
Drums heard from a distance sound pleasant and attractive.
idiom: दूर के ढोल सुहावने लगते हैं

definitions: A guess or conjecture made without calculation or sufficient evidence.
Children; infants; one's offspring or young members of a family.
In close association with or alongside something in position, status, or influence; adjacent in a non-physical sense.
Figurative: to act in one's own self-interest or to manipulate circumstances so as to secure personal benefit, often at others' expense.
Something or someone regarded as insignificant, worthless, or of no consequence; treated with complete disregard.
To light oil lamps (wicks) using clarified butter (ghee), typically as part of a ritual, offering, or devotional practice.
An utter fool; a completely stupid or gullible person.
Figurative: regret or remorse after an irreversible loss is pointless; it is too late to remedy the harm once it has occurred.
idiom: अब पछताए क्या होत जब चिड़िया चुग गई खेत

definitions: Things that are distant or unattainable often seem better than they actually are; the grass is greener on the other side.
idiom: दूर के ढोल सुहावने लगते हैं

definitions: Figurative: People or things that will not respond to reason or persuasion and can be influenced only by force, punishment, or harsh measures.
idiom: लातों के भूत बातों से नहीं मानते

definitions: Literal (noun): food or a meal; something eaten.
To gain favour, admiration, or prominence in someone's eyes; to become well regarded.
Literal: to move the head from side to side or up and down.
To accept, welcome, or adopt willingly and enthusiastically (for example, an idea, policy, culture, or person).
Figurative: Pretending to be pious, honest, or harmless while secretly being deceitful, treacherous, or hostile.
idiom: मुँह में राम बगल में छुरी

definitions: The mutual exchange or sharing of ideas, opinions, experiences, or data between individuals or groups.
The literal act of giving and receiving things, services, or information between two or more parties.
Literal: to straighten or adjust one's owl (a literal action of arranging or setting an owl upright).
To tear, split, or rip (as of cloth, paper, or other material).
To go out under a pretext or errand, often as a discreet or socially acceptable cover for leaving home or meeting someone privately.
Literal: a wolf wearing a sheep's skin; an animal concealed beneath the hide of a sheep.
To flatter, indulge, or lavish attention on someone in order to gain favor or to honor them ceremonially.
Figurative: a short-lived period of success, happiness, or fame that is quickly followed by decline, disappointment, or misfortune.
idiom: चार दिन की चाँदनी फिर अँधेरी रात

definitions: Literally, to earn or acquire a name; to obtain a name or designation.
Figurative: a person who disguises malicious intent behind a harmless or friendly appearance; someone deceptively dangerous (equivalent to 'a wolf in sheep's clothing').
To legally adopt a child or to assume permanent parental responsibility for a child not biologically one's own.
Literally, to make a hole in the same plate in which one ate.
idiom: जिस थाली में खाना उसी में छेद करना

definitions: To be saved by a hair; to avoid physical harm or death by an extremely small margin.
Used idiomatically to denote mutual devotion, suitability as partners, or actions performed for the sake of another person (often in romantic contexts).
Literal: A person who is drowning grasps even a straw for support.
Literally, for the face to lose its freshness or colour; to look withered or pale.
Figurative: a strong urge or restlessness to do something; an itching desire or compulsion to act.
Figurative: to keep quiet about something, to refrain from commenting, revealing information, or protesting.
Figurative: a person, action, or tendency that opposes, undermines, or acts contrary to sound judgment, intelligence, or rational thought.

Figurative: used to say that someone lacking the necessary knowledge, refinement, or sensitivity cannot appreciate or judge the value or subtlety of something; equivalent to 'casting pearls before swine' in sentiment.
idiom: बंदर क्या जाने अदरक का स्वाद

definitions: Figurative: To placate, ingratiate, or curry favor by giving treats or small gifts.
Literal: a half-filled water pot (gugari) tends to spill while being carried.
Literally, to eat the jaggery of a mute person, describing a physical action with no inherent figurative content.
Literal: For no hair to be out of place; for hair to remain undisturbed.
To sit down because of physical exhaustion.
Figurative: to receive or suffer an action or treatment (often unpleasant); to be on the receiving end of something.
Literal: A person who steals words.
Tears shed by a crocodile; a literal reference to the old belief that crocodiles cry while killing or devouring their prey.
Literal: Neither fit for work nor for household tasks, yet an enemy of the grain; a literal composition of descriptors portraying uselessness and antagonism toward food.
idiom: काम का न काज का दुश्मन अनाज का

definitions: Figurative: a famous, celebrated, or outstanding person in a particular field, especially in entertainment or sport.
Figurative: to receive no benefit, advantage, or recognition from an action or situation.
To rupture or burst (used for body tissues such as tendons or other anatomical structures).
Figuratively, to suffer a humiliating or disproportionate end over something trivial; often used as a caustic wish that someone should perish in an ignominious, petty way.
idiom: चुल्लू भर पानी में डूब मरना

definitions: Vulgar usage: to be torn or ruptured during sexual activity; to denote sexual penetration causing tearing.
Literal: the son-in-law of the government (someone married into a family that holds governmental power).
If the bamboo is gone, the flute made from it cannot play.
idiom: न रहेगा बाँस न बजेगी बाँसुरी

definitions: Literal: 'Sea or ocean of words,' a compound denoting an extensive or vast collection of words.
Literal: ghee will not come out by using a straight finger; pressing with a straight finger does not extract ghee.
idiom: सीधी उंगली से घी नहीं निकलता

definitions: Figurative: absolutely true; completely accurate and beyond doubt.
A state of very deep or prolonged sleep, or a prolonged period of inactivity, unawareness, or neglect.
To reconcile with or publicly show support for someone or a group, often despite controversy or past differences.
Figurative: An informal interjection meaning 'I do not know', 'God knows', or 'who knows'; also used as a mild oath or to indicate uncertainty.
Literal: To cut, slice, or sever with an edge or blade; to divide something into parts by cutting.
Figurative: to flatter, cajole, or ingratiate oneself with someone by offering excessive praise or compliments; to 'butter up' someone in order to gain favor.
Literal: A strip or band of red cloth or ribbon used as a tie, marker, or decorative strip.
To feel proud, confident, or self-assured; to swell with pride or boastfulness.
For one another; intended for or in relation to each other.

Figurative: to dress, adorn, or present an older person (typically a woman) in flashy or youthful styles in a manner considered inappropriate or incongruous; used to criticize vain or ill-suited attempts to make someone appear younger or more attractive.
To thread pearls onto a hair (literally describing an extremely delicate or precise physical action).
An insincere or hypocritical display of sorrow or sympathy; feigned grief intended to deceive.
Someone who, after suffering a bad experience, becomes excessively cautious or fearful in similar situations.
idiom: दूध का जला छाछ भी फूँक फूँक कर पीता है

definitions: A person or thing that is strongly opposed to something; an unyielding or vehement adversary.
A narrative or story concerning Rama, the mythological figure from the Ramayana.
Literally, if a desire or intent exists, a path or route will be present to reach the objective.
Figurative: to agree unquestioningly or sycophantically; to flatter or curry favor by echoing another's views.
To make a guess or take a shot in the dark (as an action).
Things that are distant or unfamiliar often seem better or more appealing than those that are close or familiar; similar to 'the grass is greener on the other side.'
Figurative: to indicate agreement, assent, or acquiescence by nodding; to signal consent or compliance nonverbally.
Literal: let the skin be lost but not the small coin; an expression that contrasts bodily loss with retaining money.
idiom: चमड़ी जाए पर दमड़ी न जाए

definitions: Beside or next to; at the side of something or someone.
To be put to death by hanging; to be executed by hanging.
Figurative: an informal epithet for an ostentatious or pretentious performer or a once-notable person now regarded as a has-been; used disparagingly to indicate affected showiness or faded talent.
To intensify or rise sharply (of temperature, emotions such as anger, or a measured quantity).
Figurative: People who make bold threats or boast loudly often fail to take effective action; loud talkers seldom deliver results.
idiom: जो गरजते हैं वो बरसते नहीं

definitions: To make or create an owl; taken literally, to cause an owl to exist or appear.
Figurative: to receive an unexpected windfall, stroke of good luck, or a valuable thing by chance.
Literal: the elephant's teeth (tusks) serve different purposes for eating and for display.
idiom: हाथी के दांत खाने के और दिखाने के और

definitions: Literal: to sing together with someone; to join another person or group in vocal performance.
Figurative: A visible token, emblem, or minor ornament that denotes affiliation, distinction, protection, or ceremonial status.
Darkness beneath a lamp; the literal contrast of a light source failing to illuminate its immediate surroundings.
Literally, to 'cut' or carve a 'चूतिया' (the offensive term for a fool); this literal sense is rare and mostly descriptive.
Figurative: a proverb used to debate or mock whether intellect or brute strength/power prevails; often used to suggest that practical force, size, or wealth may outweigh intelligence.
Literal: a luminous celestial body visible in the night sky; a star in the astronomical sense.
A member of the Tughlaq dynasty or a reference to rulers of that medieval Delhi sultanate (proper noun).
Literal: to move one's hands and feet; to have the limbs functioning and able to move.
Literal: to perform the ritual of aarti, involving the waving of a lamp or light before a deity while singing or reciting devotional verses.
Figurative: to suspect that something is amiss; to believe that there is something dishonest, hidden, or suspicious in a situation.
Figurative: A strong, decisive, and crushing response or retaliation, verbal or otherwise, that leaves the opponent defeated or silenced.
Figurative (euphemistic): To dispose of someone or something, often implying elimination or getting rid of (can be used to mean killing or permanently removing).
When dealing with a problem or punishing wrongdoing, innocents or minor elements may suffer alongside the guilty; collateral damage or undesired consequences that affect the good with the bad.
idiom: गेहूँ के साथ घुन भी पिसता है

definitions: A very small or inadequate amount relative to a great need; a token or insufficient contribution.
Figurative: A comprehensive dictionary, thesaurus, or treasury of vocabulary; by extension, a work or person possessing an exceptionally large lexicon.
Literal: truth does not burn in fire.
Figuratively, to show disappointment, dejection, or loss of enthusiasm; for one's expression to fall.
Figurative: to be severely rebuked, humiliated, or decisively defeated in a confrontation or dispute.
Literal: for there to be something black in the lentils; for the food to contain an unexpected dark spot or impurity.
Figurative: worth the money; providing good value for the amount spent (often used to praise entertainment, purchases, or services).
Literal: to eat (food); to take a meal.

Figurative: In desperate situations, even a small, inadequate aid or a last-ditch effort is seized upon; a slight or minimal help may be valuable to someone in extreme need (equivalent to 'the last straw' or 'a drowning man will clutch at a straw').
To flee, abscond, disappear, or leave suddenly and quietly; to make a swift getaway.
Literally, to drown in a handful of water; to die by drowning in a very small quantity of water.
idiom: चुल्लू भर पानी में डूब मरना

definitions: A literal description of a mulla's run terminating at the mosque.
Figurative: to support or remain with someone continuously; to be present for someone at every moment.
To overlap or lie on top of something else; to be placed one upon another.
To leave suddenly, escape, or abscond; to get away quickly.
A cumin seed in a camel's mouth; literally, the image of a tiny object inside a large mouth.
Figurative: To offer sweets, refreshments, or treats to celebrate an occasion or to please someone.
Literal: not to be touched, affected, or even to receive the fringe/edge (used less commonly in literal physical contexts).
A literal assertion that heaven (or paradise) is attainable only after death.
Figurative: a person who benefits from political favoritism or nepotism; someone who receives undue advantages, protection, or privileges because of close ties to those in power.
The literal deep sleep attributed to Kumbhakarna, a mythological giant from the Ramayana who slept for long periods.
To do anything one can, including actions one would normally avoid, out of desperation, necessity, or lack of alternatives.
Figurative (flirtatious): to make a playful or suggestive signal with the eye to show attraction or flirtation.
Figurative: at all hours; continually; throughout the day and night.
To know a specific detail such as time, place, or procedure (to have knowledge of when or how something should occur).
To help someone narrowly avoid disaster or serious trouble.
Literal: Those who make loud thunder-like noises do not produce rain.
idiom: जो गरजते हैं वो बरसते नहीं

definitions: To identify and make clear what is true and what is false, or to render a decisive, just resolution that separates right from wrong.
idiom: दूध का दूध और पानी का पानी

definitions: Figurative: To nitpick or find fault repeatedly and unnecessarily.
Literally, the name is large and the appearance is small; a literal juxtaposition of 'big name' and 'small sight'.
Figurative: For nothing to go wrong; to remain unhurt or unaffected; to experience no loss or trouble.
Figurative: one cannot expect good or desirable results from unsuitable origins or causes; outcomes correspond to what is sown or the nature of their source (equivalent to 'you reap what you sow').
idiom: बोया पेड़ बबूल का तो आम कहाँ से होए

definitions: A person or ruler who acts with capricious, eccentric, or disastrously inconsistent policies; someone whose decisions produce chaotic or harmful consequences.
Literal: for small objects or trinkets (literally 'whistles and small things') to become lost or go missing.
To move ahead of or surpass someone or something (as in overtaking or progressing beyond).
Literal: an old mare fitted with a bright red bridle; a descriptive image of an elderly horse wearing conspicuous ornamentation.
Without tears; tearless.
A person or thing of extraordinary beauty, radiance, or charm, likened to the sight of the Eid moon.
A rhetorical question used to express surprise, concern, confusion, or disbelief about a situation.
As I was saying; used to introduce or repeat a previously stated idea.
idiom: जैसा कि मैं कह रहा था

definitions: To become demoralized or disheartened and cease making further effort; to give up due to fatigue or discouragement.
Figurative: To remove or omit a part from a text, image, recording, or other work; to edit out.
To swell or become congested (of a vein, organ, or body part); to bulge or become raised.
Literal: to be seized or grabbed suddenly; to come into someone's hand with a quick, abrupt motion.
Figurative: a very stingy or miserly person; someone extremely reluctant to spend or share money or resources.
Figurative (control/possession): to be under someone's control, authority, or responsibility; to be in someone's possession or custody.
Figurative: what is true withstands scrutiny and criticism; a truthful claim or genuine innocence has nothing to fear and will remain unshaken under examination.
A person with a narrow outlook and limited experience who is unaware of the wider world; someone provincial or short-sighted in perspective.
A disparaging attitude or remark made by someone who belittles something they cannot have or achieve; an expression of sour envy or rationalization ('sour grapes').
Figurative: to indicate refusal, disagreement, or negation by shaking the head (usage varies by cultural and regional conventions).
To suffer public humiliation, disgrace, or a loss of reputation.
A rhetorical expression of frustration used when the speaker believes the listener does not understand or is pretending not to understand; equivalent to asking 'Am I speaking a foreign language?'
idiom: क्या मैं फ़ारसी बोल रहा हूँ

definitions: To deliberately worsen another person's suffering or humiliation by adding an action or remark that increases their pain or distress; equivalent to 'add insult to injury.'
Literally, to separate milk from water; to distinguish pure milk from water when mixed.
idiom: दूध का दूध और पानी का पानी

definitions: Literal: literally 'fly-sucked' or bitten/affected by flies; a descriptive compound of 'fly' and 'sucked.'
To perform a task with extreme care, delicacy, or painstaking precision; to attend to very fine or meticulous details.
Novelty is short-lived and fleeting, whereas established or familiar things endure longer.
idiom: नया नौ दिन पुराना सौ दिन

definitions: To save someone by a hair; to rescue another person from harm or death at the very last moment.
A proverb expressing that pressing needs or problems drive people to devise new solutions, innovations, or inventions.
Literal: to receive both the fruit and the value associated with its pits (used as the source image for the proverb).
idiom: आम के आम गुठलियों के दाम

definitions: Literal: literally, an 'enemy of intellect' or an adversary of reason and wisdom.
Figurative: one cannot obtain desired results through naive, blunt, or purely straightforward means; success often requires skill, tact, persistence, or indirect methods.
idiom: सीधी उंगली से घी नहीं निकलता

definitions: A proverbial expression meaning to obtain two benefits from a single action or to gain double advantage from one source.
idiom: आम के आम गुठलियों के दाम

definitions: Figurative: To take advantage of a favorable situation or prevailing trend; to profit from circumstances with little effort or risk.
Literal: Having the name of the deity Ram on the lips while carrying a knife under the arm.
idiom: मुँह में राम बगल में छुरी

definitions: For water and land to be one or joined together.
Literal: a personal name; the name of a fictional character or title (for example, used as a film or character name).
For the chest to expand or swell physically.
Where there is will or determination, a way or means will be found to achieve the desired goal.
A conversational filler employed to convey doubt, resignation, or that something is unknowable or unpredictable by humans.
A figurative term for a treacherous or deceitful person who betrays trust while pretending to be a friend; a hidden enemy.
Literal: to give breath; to grant life or vitality.
To reprimand or rebuke someone severely; to tell someone off bluntly and harshly.
Literal: to be struck or slapped on the side; a phrase describing a physical blow delivered to the flank.
Emotionlessly stoic or uncomplaining; showing little or no outward grief or sentiment.
To express a great deal in very few words; to convey much information or meaning concisely and effectively.
Figurative: to plead or beg humbly; to entreat someone for mercy, forgiveness, or favor.
Figurative: An observation that when the parties directly involved concur, outside authority or intervention is ineffective or unnecessary.
idiom: मियाँ बीवी राज़ी तो क्या करेगा काज़ी

definitions: The thin crescent moon whose sighting marks the end of Ramadan and the beginning of the festival of Eid.
Figurative: to become confused, flustered, or mentally distracted so that one cannot think or act clearly.
Literal: A reply intended to figuratively 'break the mouth' of the opponent, i.e., to silence or overwhelm them.
A rare or long‑awaited occurrence that appears after difficulty or a long interval; something that is hard to see or obtain.
Describing a person, institution, or scheme that has a grand reputation or grandiose title but little real substance, presence, benefit, or manifestation.
To take into one's lap; to hold a child, animal, or object on one's knees as a physical action of holding or cradling.
A blind attempt or random try made without preparation or information.
To become aware, conscious, or alert about an issue; to experience an intellectual, social, spiritual, or moral awakening.
Figurative: to undergo emotional rupture or extreme distress; to become heartbroken or detached.
Literal: A pairing of conjunctions meaning 'but' or 'however'; used to introduce contrast or qualification.
To accomplish two objectives with a single action; to serve two purposes at once.
A period of tranquility or apparent normalcy that immediately precedes imminent trouble, crisis, or upheaval (the 'calm before the storm').
Literal: not all five fingers are equal in size or shape.
To masturbate (typically with the hand); a coarse slang expression for self-stimulation.
To become awake or roused from sleep; to enter a state of wakefulness.
One path, two tasks; literally, a single way that addresses two matters.
A physical sensation of burning, stinging, or irritation on the skin or in an organ, typically caused by heat, chemical exposure, acid reflux, or inflammation.
Literally, 'if one does not know how to dance, the courtyard is crooked.'
A frog that lives in a well and therefore has a confined view of the outside world.
Literal: to climb or ascend onto something.
Figurative (sight): The ability to see or eyesight; used to refer to vision.
Literal: to apply butter to food or to spread butter.
Dependents or household members collectively; people who rely on someone for care or support.
To feel strength, pressure, or force in the thigh (literally referring to muscular sensation in the upper leg).
Literal: 'Happy with bread and lentils'—contentment with simple, basic food.
To create an appearance of abundance or hospitality despite actual scarcity; to pretend to have resources one does not possess, often for show or social reputation.
idiom: घर में नहीं दाने अम्मा चली भुनाने

definitions: To subject someone to severe harassment, hardship, or humiliation; to give someone a very hard time.
A display of fake sorrow or contrition meant to mislead others; hypocritical tears.
Figurative: To be in such dire poverty or desperate circumstances that even basic necessities are compromised; to suffer acute hardship or hunger.
A proverb meaning that the wrongdoer falsely blames or scolds the authority or the innocent, equivalent to 'the pot calling the kettle black.'
Literal: an empty mind is the devil's house; a mind that is empty is likened to a dwelling place for the devil.
Figurative: A person who plagiarizes or habitually appropriates others' expressions, phrases, or ideas, presenting them as their own.
The act of riding on two boats at the same time.
Figurative (metaphorical): Enlightenment, insight, hope, or a new/clarifying perspective; used to denote intellectual, spiritual, or emotional illumination.
To be united, in complete agreement, or acting as a single entity; to achieve harmony or unification between distinct parties or elements.
To ceremonially praise, honor, or publicly extol someone or something, often in a ritualized or flattering manner.
Literal: for the breath to be affected in the nose; to feel one’s breathing obstructed.
Literal: A large quantity or great amount; many or much.
Literally, to attempt to fill a small vessel (gagar) with an ocean (sagar), an impossible or absurd physical action.
Figurative: to be carefree or unconcerned about future consequences, often after spending or giving up resources; to act recklessly secure or complacent.
Culinary: to cook by the dum method; to slow-cook or steam food in a sealed pot so it cooks in its own steam.
Literal: to close one eye briefly in a deliberate gesture; to wink.
To identify or judge something on the basis of very scant, fleeting, or unreliable evidence, analogous to trying to recognize a bird from a feather while it is flying.
Figurative (secretive): to give a covert signal indicating tacit approval, complicity, or an intended secret understanding.
Literal: To break someone's spine or back; to inflict a severe physical injury to the back.
To squeeze or press a small coin or cowrie (literally referencing a very small unit of value).
Figurative: To come to fruition, to be successfully arranged or settled, or for negotiations or plans to work out (commonly used regarding agreements, proposals, or marriage arrangements).
Literally, 'as is the king, so are the subjects'; a direct statement equating the condition of rulers with that of their people.
To board or get onto a vehicle, conveyance, or platform.
To be extremely stingy or miserly; to begrudge even the smallest amount or value.
To receive or obtain news or information about an event or person.
Literal: if the winnowing basket speaks, then the sieve with seventy‑two holes will also speak.
idiom: सूप बोले तो बोले छलनी भी बोले जिसमें बहत्तर छेद

definitions: Literally, to apply 'चूना' (lime or slaked lime), for example when preparing betel leaf (paan) or treating surfaces.
Literally, ‘goodness and asking’; a literal pairing of the words for good deeds and asking.
The behavior, character, and policies of leaders determine or shape the conduct and condition of their followers, subordinates, or society at large.
To bring someone into the right path or to set someone right; to put someone in order or correct their behavior.
A literal question asking whether the speaker is speaking the Persian language.
idiom: क्या मैं फ़ारसी बोल रहा हूँ

definitions: A unit of quality or rating used to evaluate hotels, products, services, or performances (e.g., a five-star rating).
Attempting to maintain two incompatible positions, duties, relationships, or loyalties simultaneously, often leading to failure or criticism.
Figurative: People adapt their behavior, appearance, or practices to suit local circumstances, customs, or expectations.
Figurative: To reduce, curtail, or deduct (as in budgets, allocations, services, or supplies).
An informal greeting equivalent to 'what's up?', used to ask about someone's general situation.
Literal: to sell one’s horses and go to sleep; to dispose of important assets and then rest.
Literal: The result one experiences corresponds directly to the actions one has performed.
Figurative: a person who is ordinarily weak or unremarkable can behave confidently or authoritatively in familiar surroundings or within their own sphere of influence.
idiom: अपनी गली में कुत्ता भी शेर होता है

definitions: Figurative: a person who is overly delicate, pampered, ineffectual, or cowardly; someone regarded as weak or lacking robustness.
One who has been scalded by hot milk blows even on buttermilk before drinking; a literal description of cautious behaviour after being burned.
idiom: दूध का जला छाछ भी फूँक फूँक कर पीता है

definitions: To cease sleeping and become awake; to regain consciousness from sleep.
To inquire about someone's well-being or situation; to check up on someone or find out how things are going.
Figurative: to find out, to learn, or to be informed about something (to become aware of a truth, fact, diagnosis, or situation).
Literally, the sequence of numbers nine, two, eleven; a literal reading of the phrase.

Figurative: appearances or outward shows are often different from practical utility or reality; things or people may look one way but serve another purpose or lack the substance suggested by their appearance.
idiom: हाथी के दांत खाने के और दिखाने के और

definitions: Figurative: to feel proud or self‑confident, often as a result of increased prestige, popularity, or honor.
Literal: the eight traditional watches of the day (the full division of the day into eight parts).
To go to purchase or fetch cooking oil (a literal domestic errand).
To restrain or suppress one's desires, impulses, or wishes; to force oneself to give up or postpone something wanted.
A proverb meaning that people often fail to notice problems or obvious facts that are very close to them.
Literal: to be physically in the hand or in someone's immediate physical possession.
Literal: To point out small defects or minor faults in something.
A metaphorical guide or exemplary entity that directs, inspires, or represents the highest standard in a context.
Literal: four days of moonlight followed by a dark night; a literal sequence of brief brightness then darkness.
idiom: चार दिन की चाँदनी फिर अँधेरी रात

definitions: Literal: to fold one's hands together, commonly as a gesture of greeting, prayer, or respect.
A formal designation granted by the Government of India to certain Central Public Sector Enterprises that confers enhanced financial and operational autonomy.
To experience a strong sexual urge or arousal.
Literal (verb): to eat; to consume food.
Literal/Figurative: to collect, recover, or exact money (can imply legitimate recovery or forcible/extortionate collection depending on context).
Figurative: A metaphor for an extremely effective remedy, solution, or means that reliably produces the desired result; a 'sure-fire' cure or 'silver bullet'.
Figurative: To sever, interrupt, or cut off a route, supply line, or connection; to isolate by cutting access or reinforcements.
Literally, the weevil (ghun) is ground along with the wheat, describing a milling process where both grain and pests are crushed together.
idiom: गेहूँ के साथ घुन भी पिसता है

definitions: To cease sleeping; to awaken from sleep.
To blame external circumstances or make excuses for one's own lack of skill or failure instead of accepting personal responsibility.
Used figuratively to describe an organization, person, or thing regarded as especially prestigious, valuable, or exemplary.
A new thing lasts for nine days, while an old thing lasts for a hundred days.
idiom: नया नौ दिन पुराना सौ दिन

definitions: Literal: To eat something sweet so as to make the mouth taste sweet.
A piece or portion of the liver (literal anatomical meaning).
Literal: literally 'sixteen annas true', referring to a full measure and indicating completeness.
Literal: the whole or entire (referring to the complete extent or totality of something).
Figurative (availability): to be readily available or at hand for immediate use or reference.
An insincere or hypocritical display of sorrow or regret intended to deceive others; a false show of grief.
Used to point out that one cannot abandon their essential nature, needs, or means of livelihood; it is unrealistic to expect someone to give up what sustains them.
idiom: घोड़ा घास से यारी करे तो खाए क्या?

definitions: To be extremely particular about cleanliness or order; to keep oneself or one's surroundings impeccably neat and fastidious.
idiom: नाक पर मक्खी न बैठने देना

definitions: To go out, leave, or depart from a place.
Literal: For a matter or thing to be made or to come into being.
Literal: to become known or to be discovered; information or a fact comes to light.
A personal story, account, or life saga; often used to refer to one's own experiences, hardships, or a long, sometimes sentimental, narrative about oneself.
To take advantage of someone else's misfortune or turmoil for personal gain; to involve oneself in a dangerous or unethical situation in order to benefit.
Figurative: to be spineless, vacillating, or easily swayed; to lack firmness of character and change stance to suit circumstances.
